title: Tile Calculator - Tiles Needed for Any Room
shortDescription: Calculate how many square tiles you need for a room, with a 10% waste allowance.
description: Enter your room length and width in meters and the tile side length in centimeters to find out how many square tiles you need. The result includes a 10% allowance for cuts and waste, rounded up to a whole number of tiles.
metaDescription: Free tile calculator: enter room length, width and tile size to get the number of square tiles needed, including a 10% allowance for cuts and waste.

result.label: Tiles needed
result.empty: Enter the room length, width and tile size to see how many tiles you need.

sections.heading: How the tile calculator works
paragraphs: The calculator multiplies room length by room width to get the floor area, then divides by the area of one tile. Because no room tiles perfectly, it adds a 10% allowance for cuts and breakage and rounds up to a whole number of tiles.
Tile size is the side length of a square tile in centimeters, so a 30 cm tile covers 0.3 m by 0.3 m.

sections.heading: Why include a 10% waste allowance
paragraphs: Tiles get cut to fit edges and corners, and some break during handling or installation. A standard 10% waste allowance helps ensure you buy enough and keeps spares for future repairs.

howTo.name: Measure the room
howTo.text: Measure the room length and width in meters.

howTo.name: Enter the tile size
howTo.text: Enter the side length of your square tile in centimeters.

howTo.name: Read the result
howTo.text: The calculator shows the number of tiles needed, including a 10% waste allowance.

faq.q: Does the result include extra tiles for waste?
faq.a: Yes. The calculator adds a 10% allowance for cuts and breakage and rounds up to a whole number of tiles.
It is the side length of a square tile in centimeters. A 30 cm tile measures 30 cm by 30 cm.

faq.q: Why is the result rounded up?
faq.a: You cannot buy a fraction of a tile, so the count is always rounded up to the next whole tile.

faq.q: Can I use it for walls?
faq.a: Yes. Enter the wall height and width in meters in place of room dimensions.

faq.q: Should I buy more than the result?
faq.a: The 10% allowance is usually enough, but keeping a few extra tiles for future repairs is a good idea.
